student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: Done Through Others — Leadership That Scales Beyond You
lesson.menu_desc: The causative (I had the clinic accredited; I got my team to take ownership; I had my deputy run the rounds) to talk about getting work done through others and leading at scale -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Done Through Others
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 20 -- Done Through Others: Leadership That Scales Beyond You

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
to give a task or authority to someone else instead of doing it yourself
b
to supervise work and be responsible for how it turns out
c
to give someone the authority and confidence to make their own decisions
d
required to explain your actions and answer for the results
e
to control every small detail of other people's work
f
a single point that slows an entire process down
g
to have work done by an outside company instead of your own team
h
to bring a new person into a team and get them up to speed
i
excessive rules and paperwork that slow things down
j
the time and mental capacity you have to take on more work
k
to stop handling the details so that other people can lead
l
to make someone responsible for something important

paras: For the first few years, Eliane did everything herself. She had every chart reviewed personally, got every appointment confirmed in her own hand, and signed off on the smallest purchase. She told herself this was care; in truth, she had become the bottleneck. Nothing moved unless it moved through her. She was proud of how much she carried, and quietly exhausted. The clinic could not grow past the size of one very tired founder, because she refused to delegate a single decision she believed only she could make.

The shift came from an executive coach named Oliver, who asked her one hard question: what would happen to the clinic if she took a month off? She had no answer. Over the next year he taught her to lead a different way. Instead of doing the work, she learned to have it done: she had the billing system rebuilt by an outside firm, got the new doctors properly onboarded, and had her deputy run the morning rounds. She stopped trying to micromanage every case and started to empower the people around her. She got her team to take ownership, made each lead accountable for real outcomes, and learned to step back so that others could step up.

It was not comfortable. To entrust a decision to someone else, she first had to accept that they might do it differently -- and sometimes better. She had the worst of the red tape cut, outsourced what did not need her, and slowly freed up the bandwidth for the one thing no one else could do: the science, and the vision. Today the clinic runs whether she is in the room or not. When residents ask her what leadership is, she no longer talks about working harder. She says the real work of a leader is done through others -- and that is the only way anything you build outlasts you.
reading.source: Adapted from a leadership essay on scaling through people, Done Through Others

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Eliane believes a good leader should personally review every detail, because no one else can be trusted to do the work as carefully as the founder.
Eliane learned that a leader scales by getting work done through others: by delegating, empowering people, making them accountable and stepping back, so the clinic runs whether she is there or not.
The text argues that Eliane's clinic failed because she outsourced too much and lost control of the details.

items: In her first years, Eliane did almost everything herself and became the bottleneck.
The text says she had every chart reviewed personally and signed off on the smallest purchase, and that in truth she had become the bottleneck.
Oliver told Eliane exactly which tasks to keep and which to drop.
Oliver did not hand her a list; he asked one hard question -- what would happen if she took a month off -- and then taught her to lead differently.
As part of the change, Eliane had the billing system rebuilt by an outside firm and had her deputy run the morning rounds.
The text lists both: she had the billing system rebuilt by an outside firm and had her deputy run the morning rounds.
Eliane concludes that leadership means working harder than everyone else.
She says the opposite: the real work of a leader is done through others, not by working harder.

parts: : nothing moved unless it moved through me. Then I learned to
the work instead of doing it. I had the billing
, and had my deputy run the rounds. I stopped trying to
my team to make their own calls. I made each lead
for the work only I could do.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words trace a leader learning to let go. She was the bottleneck; she learned to delegate and to outsource what did not need her; she got the new people onboarded and made each lead accountable. She stopped trying to micromanage and chose to empower the team, cut the red tape, and freed up the bandwidth to entrust the big decisions to others and step back.

bank.label: Your leadership toolkit
items: I had the clinic accredited last year.
We got the billing system rebuilt by an outside firm.
I had my deputy run the morning rounds while I was away.
I got my team to take ownership of their own cases.
I had the new doctors onboarded before the busy season.
I stopped micromanaging and learned to delegate the details.
I made each team lead accountable for real outcomes.
I had the red tape cut so decisions could move faster.

lf.title: The causative: name the pattern
items: the system rebuilt by an outside firm.
her proposal turned down twice before it passed.

lf.text: These are causatives -- you arrange for someone else to do something. A + B use a thing: have or get + object + past participle (had the clinic accredited / got the system rebuilt). C + D use a person: have + person + base verb, with no 'to' (had my deputy run), but get + person + to + infinitive (got my team to take). E shows the same have + object + past participle used for something that happened to you (had her proposal turned down). The subject arranges or receives the action; someone else actually does it.

items.situation: Name one thing you arranged for someone else to do at work last month. Use have or get + object + past participle.
tips: I had the new contracts translated before the meeting.
We got the lab results digitized by an outside company.

items.situation: Describe a task you directed a specific person to handle. Use have + person + base verb (no 'to').
tips: I had my coordinator handle the whole schedule.
I had my deputy run the rounds while I was in surgery.

items.situation: Describe how you persuaded your team to change something. Use get + person + to + infinitive.
tips: I got the residents to make their own first calls.
I got the team to take ownership of their outcomes.

items.situation: Say one thing you delegate or outsource now that you used to do yourself, and why. Use two of the new words.
tips: I outsource the billing now, so I can free up bandwidth for the science.
I delegate scheduling and I no longer micromanage the details.

items: Describe a time you were the bottleneck. What were you doing yourself that you should have had done by someone else? Use have or get + object + past participle.
Choose one responsibility and hand it to a specific person. Use have + person + base verb, then get + person + to + infinitive for something you had to persuade them to do.
Name one thing you learned to delegate, outsource or entrust. Explain how it freed up your bandwidth, using at least two of the new words.
Put it together in one minute: how you stopped micromanaging, empowered the team, made them accountable, and stepped back so the work is now done through others.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: The bottleneck: 'For years I had every chart reviewed personally and signed off on everything myself -- I was the bottleneck, so nothing moved unless it moved through me.'
Directing a person: 'I had my deputy run the morning rounds, and when the team resisted, I got them to take ownership of their own cases.'
Letting go: 'I had the billing outsourced and the red tape cut, which freed up the bandwidth to entrust the big decisions to the people I trained.'
Leadership done through others: 'I stopped micromanaging, empowered each lead, made them accountable, and stepped back -- now the clinic runs whether I am in the room or not.'
answerkey.note: Notice the two causative patterns: have or get + object + past participle for a thing (had the billing outsourced), and have + person + base verb / get + person + to + infinitive for a person (had my deputy run / got them to take). You arrange the action; someone else does it.

listenings.text: Eliane, it is Oliver. I watched you in the clinic today, and I have to be honest with you as your coach. You are still the bottleneck. You had three people waiting outside your office for a signature that any of them could have given. Here is what I want you to try this month. Pick one system and have it rebuilt by someone else entirely. Take the billing, and get it outsourced. Then take the morning rounds, and have your deputy run them without you in the room. It will feel wrong. You will want to step back in and fix every detail. Do not. Your job now is not to do the work. It is to get good people to own it, and to hold them accountable for the result. Empower them, cut the red tape, and free up your bandwidth for the one thing no one else can do. Call me when you have let something go.

When new residents ask me what leadership is, I tell them what it is not. For years I thought it meant doing everything myself. I had every chart reviewed by me, I signed off on every order, and I was quietly proud of how much I carried. I was also the reason nothing could grow. The hardest lesson of my career was learning to have the work done instead of doing it. I had the systems rebuilt, I got the new doctors onboarded, and I had my deputy run the rounds. I made each lead accountable, and I forced myself to step back and let them decide. It was uncomfortable to entrust a decision to someone who might do it differently. But that is the whole job. The real work of a leader is done through others, and that is the only way anything you build outlasts you.
